steps.title: Check Statement II using SAS
steps.content:
Statement II gives $AC=DF$. Combine that with the information already given:
  • $AB=DE=6$
  • $\angle A=\angle D=40^\circ$
  • $AC=DF$
The equal angle is between the two equal sides in each triangle, so this is SAS congruence. Statement II is sufficient.

steps.title: Check Statement III using Law of Cosines
steps.content:
Statement III claims $BC = EF$. Let's apply the Law of Cosines to both triangles:

In $\triangle ABC$: $$BC^2 = AB^2 + AC^2 - 2 \cdot AB \cdot AC \cdot \cos(40¬į) = 36 + AC^2 - 12 AC \cos(40¬į)$$
In $\triangle DEF$: $$EF^2 = DE^2 + DF^2 - 2 \cdot DE \cdot DF \cdot \cos(40¬į) = 36 + DF^2 - 12 DF \cos(40¬į)$$
If $BC = EF$, then $BC^2 = EF^2$, which gives:
$$AC^2 - 12 AC \cos(40¬į) = DF^2 - 12 DF \cos(40¬į)$$
Rearranging: $(AC - DF)(AC + DF - 12\cos(40¬į)) = 0$

This equation has two possible solutions: either $AC = DF$ OR $AC + DF = 12\cos(40¬į) \approx 9.19$.

steps.title: Counterexample for Statement III
steps.content:
The second case ($AC + DF \neq AC = DF$) allows non-congruent triangles. For instance:

Suppose $\triangle ABC$ has angles $A = 40¬į, B = 40¬į, C = 100¬į$ and $AB = 6$.
Suppose $\triangle DEF$ has angles $D = 40¬į, E = 60¬į, F = 80¬į$ and $DE = 6$.

Using the Law of Sines, both triangles can be constructed with:
$$BC = \frac{6\sin(40¬į)}{\sin(100¬į)} \approx 3.92, \qquad EF = \frac{6\sin(40¬į)}{\sin(80¬į)} \approx 3.92$$
Since $\sin(100¬į) = \sin(80¬į)$, these are equal. But $AC \approx 3.92$ (because $\triangle ABC$ is isosceles with $A = B = 40¬į$) and $DF \approx 5.28$, so $AC \neq DF$.

These triangles satisfy all given conditions plus $BC = EF$, yet they are not congruent because their angles differ. Statement III is not sufficient.

steps.title: Check Statement I
steps.content:
Statement I claims angle $B = $ angle $C$ in $\triangle ABC$.

This means $\triangle ABC$ is isosceles with:
$$40¬į + B + C = 180¬į \implies B = C = 70¬į$$
So $\triangle ABC$ has angles $40¬į‚Äď70¬į‚Äď70¬į$. However, this tells us nothing about $\triangle DEF$. We only know $D = 40¬į$ and $DE = 6$ for triangle $DEF$; angles $E$ and $F$ are unrestricted (as long as $E + F = 140¬į$). Without knowing whether $\triangle DEF$ also has the $40¬į‚Äď70¬į‚Äď70¬į$ angle configuration, we cannot prove congruence. Statement I is not sufficient.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Summary:
  • Statement I ‚ÄĒ Not sufficient (defines angles in $ABC$ only, no constraint on $DEF$)
  • Statement II ‚ÄĒ Sufficient (SAS congruence)
  • Statement III ‚ÄĒ Not sufficient (ambiguous case; non-congruent triangles possible)
Only Statement II guarantees congruence. That matches choice B ‚ÄĒ B is correct.
